context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:
Course context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 4

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 4


Problem 4.1 (25 pts)

In the beaded string shown in Figure 1, the interval between neighboring beads is a, and the distance from the end beads to the wall is a/2. All the beads have mass m and are constrained to move only vertically in the plane of the paper. The strings are massless with constant string tension T.

[Figure 1: Beads on a string — beads numbered 1, 2, 3, ..., N−1, N spaced a apart, with the end beads a distance a/2 from each fixed wall.]

(a) Show that the physics of the left-hand wall can be incorporated by going to an infinite system and requiring the boundary condition A1 = −A0.
(b) Find the analogous boundary condition for the right-hand wall.
(c) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies for the finite system.


Problem 4.2 (25 pts)

A physicist was trying to understand a system with two coupled torsional pendula. First, she tried to write down the equations of motion of the pendula in terms of θ1 and θ2, which are the angles with respect to the equilibrium positions of the pendula. She found that the interaction matrix M^(−1)K commutes with the 2 × 2 reflection symmetry matrix
    S = [ 0  −1 ]
        [ −1  0 ]
(i.e., satisfies the condition [S, M^(−1)K] = 0).

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) What will be the amplitude ratio (A1/A2, where A1 and A2 are the components of the eigenvector) of the torsional pendula in each normal mode?


Problem 4.3 (25 pts)

Consider a uniform thin string of length L and mass density µ. The string is attached at both ends to vertical, frictionless rods via massless rings as shown in Figure 2. The tension in the string is T.

[Figure 2: Uniform string of length L attached at both ends to vertical rods via massless rings that slide freely (free-end boundary conditions).]

(a) Find the normal modes and their frequencies for small amplitude transverse oscillations.
(b) Sketch the shapes of the first three normal modes.
(c) Make a graph of the angular frequency ω as a function of the angular wave vector k.


Problem 4.4 (25 pts)

A string of mass density µ and stretched with tension T is deformed as shown in Figure 3 (amplitude A is very small; the deformation as shown in the figure is greatly exaggerated). The string is released at t = 0 with zero initial velocity (∂y(x,t)/∂t = 0 at t = 0 for all x).

[Figure 3: String deformation — a triangular shape on the interval [−L, L], equal to 0 at x = −L, rising to +A at x = −L/2, passing through 0 at x = 0, dropping to −A at x = L/2, and returning to 0 at x = L.]

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) Find expressions for the Fourier coefficients Am of the normal mode expansion of the initial string shape and use them to write a full time-dependent series that describes the motion of the string at t > 0: y(x,t).
(c) Make a sketch of the amplitudes Am of the modes as a function of mode number m. Make sure that you indicate amplitudes for all possible normal modes of the string.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on Problem Set 4 for 8.03SC, on normal modes and waves. I’m most confused about the beaded string boundary conditions in 4.1, especially why the wall gives something like \(A_1=-A_0\).
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's a natural place to start. Let me ask you this: what does the fixed wall actually require of the string at that boundary? What is the displacement of the string at the wall itself?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a specific conceptual difficulty: why the boundary condition at the wall for the beaded string is A1 = -A0. Rather than explaining this directly, I should use the Socratic method to guide them to think about what the wall actually does physically, and what condition the wall enforces on the string's displacement. A single targeted question is the right starting point.

exchanges.student: I think the wall means the displacement there has to be zero. I’m just not totally seeing how that turns into a condition on the bead amplitudes instead of directly on the wall point.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — zero displacement at the wall is the key. Now, the wall isn't sitting on a bead; it's sitting between the imaginary bead 0 and real bead 1, at the midpoint between them. If you imagine the displacement varying smoothly, what condition on A0 and A1 would force the displacement to be zero right at that midpoint?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that the displacement at the wall must be zero. Now I need to guide them to connect that physical condition to the mathematical trick of the 'image' bead A0. The key insight is that the wall sits exactly halfway between bead 0 (the imaginary bead) and bead 1 (the first real bead), so if the displacement at the wall's position must be zero, what must be true about A0 and A1? I'll ask them to think about where the wall sits relative to the beads.

exchanges.student: Maybe the midpoint displacement should be the average, so \((A_0 + A_1)/2 = 0\)? If that’s allowed, then I get \(A_1 = -A_0\), but I’m not sure if taking the midpoint as the average is the right move here.
exchanges.tutor: You got the right result, but let's sharpen the reasoning. Instead of averaging, think about the actual form of the normal mode: the displacement of bead n goes as sin(kna) or cos(kna). The wall is located at a specific position — can you figure out exactly where bead 0 would sit relative to bead 1 and the wall, and then ask what the sinusoidal solution must equal at the wall's position?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the right answer A1 = -A0 but is uncertain about the justification. The 'average' reasoning is intuitive but not quite the right physical argument. The better argument is that in the extended infinite system, the wall position itself must have zero displacement, and in a normal mode the displacement at any point between beads can be written as a sinusoidal function. The wall is at the midpoint, so interpolating linearly (or using the actual wave solution) gives zero there if A0 = -A1. I should nudge them toward thinking about what the normal mode solution looks like spatially — it's a sinusoid — and what that sinusoid must satisfy at the wall position, rather than just averaging.

exchanges.student: I think bead 1 is at \(x=a/2\) if I put the left wall at \(x=0\), so the imaginary bead 0 would be at \(x=-a/2\). Then the wall is exactly halfway between them. If the mode is something like \(A(x)\), I guess I need \(A(0)=0\), and that should make the values at \(\pm a/2\) opposite, so \(A_1=-A_0\).
exchanges.tutor: That reasoning is solid — you've got it. The wall at x=0 demands zero displacement, and the antisymmetry of the sinusoid around that point gives you A1 = -A0 cleanly. Now can you apply the same logic to the right-hand wall to find the boundary condition there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly reasoned through the geometry and arrived at A1 = -A0 with solid justification — the wall at x=0 requires zero displacement, and the imaginary bead 0 at x=-a/2 is the mirror image of bead 1 at x=a/2, giving opposite amplitudes. This is exactly the right argument. I should affirm this clearly and then push them forward to part (b), the right-hand wall boundary condition, which is the natural next step.

exchanges.student: I think the right wall is halfway between bead \(N\) and an imaginary bead \(N+1\), so maybe it’s the same pattern: \(A_{N+1} = -A_N\). Is that the whole boundary condition for the right side?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's correct. Now you have both boundary conditions. For part (c), can you think about what form the amplitudes An must take for a normal mode, and how you'd apply both boundary conditions to find the allowed values of k?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the boundary condition for the right wall by analogy. This is good progress. I should affirm this and move them toward part (c), finding the normal modes and frequencies, which is the most substantive part of the problem.

exchanges.student: I think I should try \(A_n = C e^{ikna} + D e^{-ikna}\), or maybe just a sine form after using the left boundary. I’m a little stuck on how to use both \(A_1=-A_0\) and \(A_{N+1}=-A_N\) to quantize \(k\).
exchanges.tutor: Good start. Apply the left boundary condition A1 = -A0 to your general form first — what does that tell you about C and D? That will simplify things before you tackle the right wall.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with the general form. They correctly anticipate that the left boundary condition will simplify things. I should guide them to first apply the left boundary condition A1 = -A0 to their general form to determine the relationship between C and D (which should give a sine form), and then apply the right boundary condition to quantize k. I'll ask them to take it one step at a time — apply the left BC first.

exchanges.student:
Let me try it. From \(A_n = C e^{ikna} + D e^{-ikna}\),

\[
A_0 = C + D,\qquad A_1 = Ce^{ika} + De^{-ika}.
\]

Then \(A_1=-A_0\) gives

\[
Ce^{ika} + De^{-ika} = -(C+D).
\]

I’m not immediately seeing the clean relation between \(C\) and \(D\), unless this is supposed to turn into a sine shifted by \(a/2\)?
exchanges.tutor: You're close. Since the wall is at x=0 and bead n is at position x = (n − 1/2)a, what happens if you try the form A_n = B sin(k(n − 1/2)a) directly? Does it automatically satisfy the left boundary condition?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track but struggling to see the simplification. The hint about sine shifted by a/2 is actually the key insight. Rather than solving the algebra directly for them, I should guide them to try a specific substitution. The cleanest approach is to write An as a sine centered at the wall position x=0, i.e., An = B sin(kna) where bead positions are at x = (n-1/2)a... but actually the cleaner way given the geometry (wall at x=0, bead 1 at x=a/2) is An = B sin(k * x_n) where x_n = (n-1/2)a, or equivalently An = B sin(k(n - 1/2)a). Let me guide the student to think about trying a sine form centered on the wall directly.

exchanges.student: Oh, I think that helps. If \(A_n = B\sin(k(n-\tfrac12)a)\), then at the wall \(x=0\) the sine is zero automatically, so yeah, that seems to build in the left boundary condition. Then I guess I should plug in \(n=N\) and \(n=N+1\) to use \(A_{N+1}=-A_N\), but I’m not sure what trig step to use there.
exchanges.tutor: Good. Write out A_{N+1} and A_N explicitly using your sine form, set up the equation A_{N+1} = -A_N, and then think: what condition on k makes that equation true? Hint — can you use a sum formula to expand sin(k(N+1/2)a)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the sine form and sees that the left BC is automatically satisfied. Now they need to apply the right BC. I should guide them to write out A_{N+1} and A_N explicitly using the sine form and set up the equation A_{N+1} = -A_N, then think about what trig identity or condition makes that equation true.

exchanges.student:
Okay, let me try:  
\[
A_N = B\sin\!\big(k(N-\tfrac12)a\big), \qquad A_{N+1}=B\sin\!\big(k(N+\tfrac12)a\big).
\]
Then the boundary condition gives
\[
\sin\!\big(k(N+\tfrac12)a\big)=-\sin\!\big(k(N-\tfrac12)a\big).
\]
Using \(\sin(\alpha+\beta)=\sin\alpha\cos\beta+\cos\alpha\sin\beta\), I feel like this should collapse to something like \(\sin(kNa)\cos(ka/2)=0\), but I’m not fully confident.
exchanges.tutor: Your trig manipulation looks right — verify it carefully, but that form is correct. Now, cos(ka/2) = 0 would give very large k values inconsistent with the discrete system, so which factor must vanish? What does that tell you about the allowed values of k?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is very close. They correctly expanded using the sum formula and are nearly at the right condition. I should confirm their trig manipulation is on the right track and ask them to interpret what condition sin(kNa)cos(ka/2)=0 places on k, and which solution is physically meaningful.

exchanges.student:
Then it must be \(\sin(kNa)=0\), so

\[
kNa = m\pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_m = \frac{m\pi}{Na}.
\]

I’m a bit unsure about which integers \(m\) are allowed though — probably \(m=1,2,\dots,N\)?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on the allowed values — good reasoning. Now you have the normal modes. To finish part (c), what's the dispersion relation for the beaded string that connects k to ω? Use it to write down the normal mode frequencies ω_m.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly found the quantization condition and the right range of m. I should confirm their answer on the allowed values and briefly prompt them to now connect k_m to the dispersion relation to get the frequencies — completing part (c).

grade.overview: The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning throughout all 10 turns, guiding the student step-by-step through the beaded string boundary condition problem without giving away answers. Each turn built on the student's prior response, scaffolding was well-structured, and the tutor stayed tightly anchored to the assignment. Responses were appropriately concise and the tone was supportive throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted. The tutor never gave a final answer or submission-ready reasoning; instead, each turn posed a targeted question or hint that kept the student doing the key reasoning work (e.g., deriving A1=-A0, choosing the sine form, applying the trig identity, identifying which factor vanishes). Scaffolding was progressive and built directly on student contributions. The tutor correctly diagnosed where the student was stuck before responding. Dialogue was efficient with no repetition or off-topic drift. Responses were bite-sized and clear, and the tone was consistently encouraging without implying formal grading.
